coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots with two-photon emission for ultrasensitive detection of tumor marker and visual monitor anticancer drug loading and delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Peiwei
Sun, Lu
Wang, Fei
Liu, Xicheng
Yan, Zhengquan
Wang, Mengzhen
Zhang, Lei
Tian, Zhenzhen
Liu, Zhe
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: Early diagnosis and therapy are practically important to effectively prevent cancer and improve patients’ life quality. Herein, a simple and sensitive two-photon imaging fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) platform was constructed to detect β-glucuronidase (βG, a tumor-invasive biomarker) based on inner filter effect (IFE), to visually monitor anticancer drug loading by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and to effectively treat cancer by chemotherapy. To build this sensing platform, novel N-CDs with strong green emission (Ca. QY = 45%) were synthesized by one-pot pyrolysis reaction, and specifically, careful structure design of the excitation bands of N-CDs well covered the absorption bands of p-nitrophenol (PNP, βG catalytic product), making it ultra-sensitive towards the activity of βG and showing good linear relationship from 1 to 15 U/L and exciting detection limit of 0.3 U/L (S/N = 3). This sensing platform has been successfully employed for βG sensing in both human serum and intracellular detection. Moreover, we also demonstrated the first application of N-CDs as a novel visual detection sensor to monitor the loading process of doxorubicin, and as a nanocarrier for cancer therapy. More importantly, the N-CDs also exhibited high quality single- and two-photon fluorescence in high depth (18 μm), enabling the N-CDs to real-time monitor the endocytosis, intracellular distribution and release of drugs, indicating significant potential for biomedicine and bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
β-Glucuronidase
Nanocarrier
Chemotherapy

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of βG activity in human serum samples and cells
DOX loading under the detection of UV and fluorescence spectrophotometer
Intracellular DOX uptake and cell imaging by both SPFI and TPFI
Structure and surface chemistry of N-CDs
Fluorescence detection of βG activity based on IFE in lab and human serum samples
Application to βG sensing in living cells
Visualized drug loading and chemical composition analyses
Cell uptake and drug release in HeLa cells
Single- and two-photon fluorescence imaging in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution histogram (inset) of N-CDs. Chemical composition analyses, (b) FTIR spectrum and (c) full survey XPS spectrum of N-CDs. High resolution XPS spectrum and the fitting results of (d) C1s and (e) N1s (inset shows corresponding O1s result). (f) XRD pattern of N-CDs.

(a) The black, red and blue curves represent the absorption spectrum of PNP, the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, respectively. (b) The absorbing data of enzymatic reaction solution with different contents of βG from 1 U/L to 200 U/L.

(a) Fluorescence intensities of N-CDs after added into different concentrations of βG from 0 to 200 U/L. Inset: the plot of I0-I versus βG activity. (b) The corresponding linear relationship of I0-I versus βG concentration from 1 to 15 U/L. I and I0 represented the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs with and without βG, respectively.

Real-time monitoring of enzymatic reaction, HeLa cells were treated with N-CDs for (a) bright field and (b) fluorescence field. The cells were treated with N-CDs and PNPG (1 mM) for (c) 1 h and (d) 4 h, respectively. Emission intensities were collected at 470–570 nm under the excitation of N-CDs at 405 nm. Scale bar: 20 μm.

(a) Fluorescence data of N-CDs recorded after reaction with DOX at various concentrations from 0 to 33 μg/mL. (b) Absorption spectra of N-CDs, DOX and N-CDs + DOX. (c) FTIR spectra of DOX and N-CDs + DOX.

Single- and two-photon microscope images of intracellular tracking of N-CDs + DOX with different monitoring time, including N-CDs channel, DOX channel and overlay of N-CDs and DOX channel. (a, b, c) and (e, f, g) represented single-photon fluorescence and (d and h) represented two-photon fluorescence for HeLa cell incubated with N-CDs + DOX, respectively. (All scale bars: 20 μm). The concentration of DOX was kept at 10 μg/mL.

(a) Cell viabilities of 16HBE and HeLa cell for N-CDs under different concentrations (10–200 μg/mL, 24 h). Viabilities of HeLa cell treated with N-CDs + DOX and free DOX at different concentrations (1–20 μg/mL) for (b) 24 h, (c) 48 h and (d) 72 h.

Illustration of the application of N-CDs for ultrasensitive detection of β-glucuronidase based on IFE, visually monitor anticancer drug loading by FRET and monitor drug delivery by two-photon fluorescence imaging.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots with two-photon emission for ultrasensitive detection of tumor marker and visual monitor anticancer drug loading and delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel two-photon fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dot is synthesized, and we report its first application as an effective fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive detection of β-glucuronidase in both live cells and human serum, and visual monitor of anticancer drug loading and drug delivery.

Early diagnosis and therapy are practically important to effectively prevent cancer and improve patients’ life quality. Herein, a simple and sensitive two-photon imaging fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) platform was constructed to detect β-glucuronidase (βG, a tumor-invasive biomarker) based on inner filter effect (IFE), to visually monitor anticancer drug loading by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and to effectively treat cancer by chemotherapy. To build this sensing platform, novel N-CDs with strong green emission (Ca. QY = 45%) were synthesized by one-pot pyrolysis reaction, and specifically, careful structure design of the excitation bands of N-CDs well covered the absorption bands of p-nitrophenol (PNP, βG catalytic product), making it ultra-sensitive towards the activity of βG and showing good linear relationship from 1 to 15 U/L and exciting detection limit of 0.3 U/L (S/N = 3). This sensing platform has been successfully employed for βG sensing in both human serum and intracellular detection. Moreover, we also demonstrated the first application of N-CDs as a novel visual detection sensor to monitor the loading process of doxorubicin, and as a nanocarrier for cancer therapy. More importantly, the N-CDs also exhibited high quality single- and two-photon fluorescence in high depth (18 μm), enabling the N-CDs to real-time monitor the endocytosis, intracellular distribution and release of drugs, indicating significant potential for biomedicine and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as efficient and sensitive probes and nanocarriers are gaining considerable attention for their capabilities, such as biological imaging, early diagnosis and drug delivery [1–4]. CDs can compete commercially with conventional fluorescent nanomaterials because of the former’s unusual and interesting optical properties, good dispersibility, chemical stability, low toxicity and easy synthesis [5–6]. These advantages enable CD-based fluorescent sensors to detect ions (e.g., Hg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+) [7–10], pH [11], and molecular substances [12] by monitoring changes in fluorescence signals. These advantages endow CDs with the ability to act as a nanocarrier for intracellular imaging and drug tracing. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) can reportedly enhance photoluminescence [13]. Thus, developing N-CDs to expand fluorescent CD application in fluorescent probes and drug tracing is necessary.

In cancer treatment, the early detection of tumor-invasive biomarkers contributes to the diagnosis and early treatment of malignancies and leads to higher survival opportunities for cancer patients. β-Glucuronidase (βG) is one of the lysosomal glycosides that can catalyze the degradation of the extracellular matrix of normal and cancer tissues and glycosaminoglycan on the cell membranes [14]. According to earlier reports, the increased activities of βG can induce several cancers, such as liver cancer [15], colon carcinoma [16], renal carcinoma [17], and neoplasm of the bladder [18]. The over-expression of βG in the serum was also closely related to some pathological conditions, including renal diseases, epilepsy, urinary tract infection, larynx, and breast [19–21] Therefore, the specific and sensitive determination of βG is important. However, the determination method needs further study. Recently, fluorogenic substrates [22], colorimetric method [23], and chemosensor with organic fluorescent probes [15] were the most commonly used quantitative detection methods. Although these methods had their own characteristics and gave different insights for βG sensing, they possess inherent disadvantages, including low detection sensitivity, poor water solubility and photostability, poor repeatability, complicated synthesis process, surface modification and high cost. Hence, developing a novel, specific, and sensitive fluorescent probe is necessary to detect βG with high sensitivity, selectivity, operability and low cost. Recently, the inner filter effect (IFE) has emerged as an effective method for sensor design, it bases on the mechanism that when the absorber’s absorption spectra overlap with the fluorophore’s excitation or emission spectra in the detection system [24,25]. IFE can transform the absorption responses into fluorescent signals; [26] it exhibits an enhanced sensitivity compared with other fluorescence-sensing mechanisms, such as electronic energy transfer (EET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), because IFE does not need surface modification of the fluorescence material or the covalent linking between the receptor and fluorophore. However, screening out a suitable absorber fluorophore pair is still a great challenge due to limitations of fluorophores.

Herein, we designed and prepared a novel N-CDs with bright green emission and which gave a high fluorescence yield of about 45% using a one-pot pyrolysis strategy. The N-CDs with nitrogen groups has been reported to give excellent optical properties and substantially expand their novel applications [27]. Therefore, we developed a facile and reliable IFE-based N-CDs fluorescence sensor platform for βG-activity sensing and the N-CDs were directly used as the fluorophore (Scheme 1 ). 4-Nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) was selected as the substrate of βG, and p-nitrophenol (PNP) as a βG reaction product was adopted as the absorber. Interestingly, the absorption wavelength of PNP was well-overlapped with the excitation wavelength of N-CDs. Therefore, the excitation of N-CDs was weakened due to the competitive absorption between N-CDs and PNP, leading to remarkable quenching of N-CDs. The proposed sensing strategy was applied to detect βG activity in serum samples and exhibited good applicability. This sensing assay provided a satisfactory accuracy of 10 μL of serum sample consumption, which was of crucial importance for clinical studies. We also investigated the possibility of βG cell imaging using the proposed fluorescence detection strategy for the first time, and this sensing platform exhibited high sensitivity and imaging effects toward βG detection in living cells [28]. Therefore, the developed fluorescence assay was proven to have many merits, such as simplicity, low toxicity, high selectivity and superior sensitivity, which exhibited broad prospect in cancer early-stage diagnosis.

Considering the unique physicochemical and luminescent properties, N-CDs were also explored as a novel nanocarrier for tumor therapy, as well as for cellular imaging and drug tracing. This drug delivery system was constructed by attaching doxorubicin (DOX), a universal anticancer drug in the clinical treatment, to N-CDs via π-π stacking interaction [29], leading to an emission intensity of N-CDs decreased based on a FRET mechanism [30] and allowing us to visually monitor the loading process. When DOX-loaded N-CDs were taken up into tumor cells, the DOX was released from the N-CDs surface, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs and giving rise to efficient killing of cancer cells [31]. More importantly, the N-CDs also exhibited strong two-photon fluorescence upon excitation at the near-infrared region (800–1100 nm) and possess the advantages of enhanced tissue penetration with a safer treatment [32–34]. Compared to single-photon fluorescence imaging (SPFI), the two-photon fluorescence imaging (TPFI) was highly desirable for biomedical imaging due to its low cell damage, large imaging depth, high resolution, and three-dimensional imaging capability [35,36]. High-quality single- and two-photon cellular imaging endows N-CDs with the capability to monitor endocytosis, drug-release, and intracellular distribution in real-time, indicating its significant potential in bioimaging and cancer therapy.
The N-CDs were prepared by using one-pot pyrolysis reaction method. The detailed information was explained in Supporting Information.

The IFE-based N-CDs fluorescent sensing for βG activity assay was performed according to the following procedures. βG was dissolved in 0.1 M pH = 5.0 NaAc-HAc buffer and then βG with activities from 0 to 200 U/L was added into PNPG solution (300 μL, 1 mM). After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the reaction solution was modulated to alkaline by adding 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution diluted with pH 8.0 PBS buffer. After 100 μL of the mixture was transferred into 1 mL as-prepared N-CDs, the fluorescence spectra were obtained with a 410 nm excitation wavelength. In order to research the selective detection of βG, interferences including amino acids and metal ions were investigated according to above procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of βG activity in human serum samples and cells
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples of six adult volunteers were collected from Qufu People's Hospital (Shandong, China) and stored frozen for use. These samples were analyzed using the proposed fluorescence detection strategy, in which βG solutions were replaced by 10 μL of serums and other procedures were performed according to the proposed βG assay. Colorimetric methods that were commonly used in clinic were also employed to analyze the serum samples [37].
βG activity in HeLa cells was investigated using the proposed fluorescence detection strategy, and cell culture and specific method of operation were explained in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading under the detection of UV and fluorescence spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para:
To load the anticancer drug, 2 mg of DOX was added into N-CDs solution (4 mL, 1 mg/mL). After continuous stirring for 24 h under the dark, the product (N-CDs + DOX) was obtained by centrifugation and lyophilization. In order to characterize DOX loading directly on N-CDs, the precipitate was re-dispersed in water, and the hybrid suspension was analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. And the DOX was quantified from measuring the absorbance at 488 nm by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer based on FRET effects. Loading capacity (LC) and loading efficiency (LE) of N-CDs + DOX were evaluated by the following equations, respectively [38],
(1)
LC
mg / mg
=
DOX
-
DOX free
N - CDs
(2)
LE
%
=
DOX
-
DOX free
DOX
× 100 %
where [DOX] represents the initial amount of DOX, [N-CDs] represents the total amount of N-CDs in N-CDs + DOX added and [DOXfree] represents the amount of DOX without load.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular DOX uptake and cell imaging by both SPFI and TPFI
ce:section.ce:para: Cell uptake and imaging of N-CDs + DOX and free DOX were studied by flow cytometry and LSM, respectively. The detailed information was displayed in Supporting Information.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the samples was investigated using the MTT assay. The culture conditions of cells and treated with samples were according to the experimental process explained in Supporting Information. The cell viability was obtained by the following equation:
(3)
CellViability
A sample
-
A blank
A control
-
A blank
× 100 %
In the equation, Asample, Ablank and Acontrol represent the absorbance of the samples, blank and control groups, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and surface chemistry of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the morphology study of N-CDs showed that the as-prepared N-CDs, displayed uniform size and good monodispersity in aqueous solution. Statistics on the size distribution showed narrow size range of N-CDs was 1.5–4.0 nm and the average size was about 3 nm. To present clearer surface element composition of N-CDs, both FTIR and XPS were carried out to characterize the resulting sample. As shown in Fig. 1b, FTIR spectrum showed two prominently absorption peaks at 3408 cm-1 and 1620 cm-1, which belonged to O–H stretching vibration and CO bonds, respectively.[39–40] CO stretching peaks at 1229 cm-1 and 1085 cm-1 can be also found, and these oxygen groups on N-CDs guarantee good dispersibility in water and other solvents (Fig. S1). Moreover, obvious CN and CNstretch bands at 1386 cm-1 and 1340 cm-1 can also be found in N-CDs. XPS study was applied to further characterize the chemical composition of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, full survey XPS spectrum of N-CDs showed three typical peaks centered at 284.9 eV (C1s), 400.4 eV (N1s), and 533 eV (O1s), indicating high purity of the products. The nitrogen content in N-CDs was estimated to be 9.38%, indicating very high nitrogen content compared to other quantum dots (Table S1). The specific chemical bond of N-CDs was analyzed through C1s and N1s XPS spectrum. The C1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1d) was fitted into four peaks represented CC (284.5 eV), CN (285.4 eV), OCO (286.7 eV) and CO (288.4 eV) groups, respectively.[41] Moreover, the peak at 400.3 eV confirmed the existence of (C)3-N group and the peak at 402 eV belonged to N–H group for N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e).[42] In addition, the XRD pattern of N-CDs (Fig. 1f) represented one peak at around 2θ = 25.3°, which corresponded to (0 0 2) lattice plane.[43] These above data indicated that our developed method are very facile and effective to prepare N-CDs enriched with both oxygen and nitrogen groups to guarantee good water solubility, and possessing uniform size to have identical properties.

To study the fluorescence properties of N-CDs, systematic investigation was carried out, and corresponding results were shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 (Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. S2a, different from the absorption peaks of hydroquinone and ethylenediamine at 225, 288 and 217 nm, the absorption peak of N-CDs were at 236 and 407 nm, indicating high purify of the products after dialysis. From the fluorescence data in Fig. S2b, the excitation peak of N-CDs was at 410 nm and the corresponding emission at 510 nm. And the N-CDs exhibited good dispersion in aqueous solutions (top inset in Fig. S2b). The photoluminescence properties of N-CDs were further explored by investigating the excitation between 310 nm and 450 nm in Fig. S3. Intriguingly, the central peak position remained nearly invariable and fluorescence intensity of N-CDs reached its maximum when the excitation wavelength at 410 nm. The excitation-independent PL behavior indicated that the N-CDs possess relatively uniform size (in Fig. 1a), which guarantee the extraordinary PL performance [44]. The uniform size and high contents of doped nitrogen ensured higher quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs, ca. 45%, a value higher than common CDs (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of βG activity based on IFE in lab and human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Very recently, IFE has been used as a novel and effective method to detect targeted analyte through the conversion of the absorbing signals into fluorescent ones [45], are attracting increasing attention because of its higher selectivity and sensitivity compared to other fluorescence sensing methods and simplicity, and requires no chemical links between receptors and fluorophores [46]. However, the main drawbacks that hinder its application is the high requirement for choosing appropriate absorber and fluorophore. Thus, the design and synthesis of a suitable fluorophore is the key process in constructing IFE-based sensors. Herein, we prepared a novel N-CDs-based fluorophore by screening different reaction materials and conditions, and the N-CDs were introduced as a fluorophore for fluorescence-sensing assay for βG activity. We also chose cheap and easily available PNPG as the substrate of βG and used PNP (βG hydrolysis product) as the IFE-absorber. This design remarkably takes advantages of the PNPG, whose absorption peak is 305 nm, which does not affect the fluorescence of N-CDs. By contrast, the absorption peak of PNP (400 nm) overlapped well with the excitation wavelength of N-CDs (410 nm, Fig. 2 a), which allowed high-sensitivity based on IFE and produced a significant effect on the fluorescence of N-CDs.

To verify this design, βG with various concentrations were incubated with PNPG, and the hydrolysate PNP was generated. The absorption spectra of this enzyme reaction were investigated after βG with various concentrations from 1 U/L to 200 U/L were added and the absorption peaks at 305 nm and 400 nm were decreased and increased gradually, respectively (Fig. 2b). This reaction caused a significant change in the solution color from colorless to yellow because of the production of PNP (Fig. S4). The enzymatic reaction based on IFE is a facile, selective, and ultrasensitive method of detecting βG activity via change in color compared with the colorimetric method.

As indicated in Fig. 3 a, after βG with various concentrations from 0 U/L to 200 U/L were added, the fluorescence intensities decreased gradually. The corresponding relative fluorescence intensity I0-I versus βG activity was recorded in the inset of Fig. 3a. Fig. 3b exhibited the I0 − I versus βG concentration plot in the range 1 U/L to 15 U/L (Y = 26.38 X + 5.29, R2 = 0.9883), indicating a very good linear relationship. Then, the proposed method offered a detection limit that was about 0.3 U/L via the generalized 3σ method, which can be used to assay βG activity in biological samples, because the βG concentration of normal adult serum was about 8.0 U/L. The performance of this method was compared with other methods (Table S3), and the detailed comparison is described in the Supporting Information. To explore the selectivity of the proposed approach, the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs was studied through ordinary interfering substances, including amino acids, a number of enzymes, and metal ions in serum. As shown in Fig. S5, only βG had a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, whereas the listed amino acids, enzymes, and metal ions presented a negligible effect under the same conditions, indicating that the proposed sensing strategy provided a satisfactory selectivity.

To further explore the analytical reliability and applicability of the method, we collected six human serum samples, including three tumor patients and three normal patients, and analyzed them using our proposed sensing method. These samples were also analyzed via the clinical methods [47]. The analytical results were presented in Fig. S6, which shows that the βG activity in cancer patient serums (17.2–19.2 U/L) were significantly higher compared with healthy patients (6.8–9.1 U/L) based on the calibration curve. After adding a certain amount of βG into the serum sample, we found the recovery rate to be more than 95% (Table S4), indicating that the proposed sensing strategy has a high accuracy. All these results suggested that the βG activity is closely related to the cancer and further served as an effective tumor biomarker. More importantly, our proposed sensing method had a very satisfactory accuracy within 10 μL serum sample consumption, which possessed great importance in clinical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to βG sensing in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the unique optical properties, studies were carried out to assess the potential of N-CDs for fluorescence imaging in cells. For this purpose, HeLa cells were treated with N-CDs at 37 °C for 30 min, and a significant green fluorescence was produced in the cytoplasm, indicating the high cellular uptake of N-CDs by HeLa cells (Fig. 4 b). After washing the cells loaded with N-CDs, 1 mM PNPG was added for βG sensing. As a result, PNPG was hydrolyzed into PNP by βG, and intracellular fluorescence was remarkably decreased due to IFE. The resulting fluorescence image (Fig. 4c) displayed a weak green fluorescence than Fig. 4b after incubating for 1 h, and after 4 h, weaker fluorescence was shown in Fig. 4d. Similar experimental results were obtained in A549 cell lines (Fig. S7), indicating the wide applicability of this method. The results of the studies indicating the IFE fluorescence strategy can expediently detect βG in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualized drug loading and chemical composition analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the novel IFE application of N-CDs for sensitive and selective detection of βG in serum samples and HeLa cells, we also developed N-CDs as an effective sensor to monitor the loading process of DOX through FRET effects. DOX is a broad-spectrum anticancer drug that has been widely used in clinic to treat cancers; it is detected on nanocarriers usually via UV–vis spectrophotometry, which often takes a long time and needs complex procedures. Taking advantage of the high water-solubility of N-CDs and their π−π conjugating interaction between DOX, we report here the first application of N-CDs as a visual detection sensor to monitor DOX loading. As shown in Fig. 5 a, with increasing amount of DOX being loaded onto N-CDs, fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased progressively. This result suggested that loading DOX onto N-CDs indeed resulted into energy transfer from N-CDs to DOX when they were in closed proximity [48]. The PL of DOX under different excitation wavelengths is provided in Fig. S8, which indicated that DOX exhibited excellent excitation-independence. Therefore, monitoring the drug loading can be achieved through the naked-eye observation of the changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S9) [49]. The successful loading process was also confirmed by the absorption and FTIR spectra. Two distinct absorption peaks were present at 407 and 488 nm for N-CDs + DOX, which revealed that DOX was successfully attached to N-CDs (Fig. 5b) [50]. The FTIR spectra provided direct evidence that DOX was loaded onto N-CDs (Fig. 5c). Compared with the DOX spectrum, two peaks appeared at 1582 and 1617 cm-1 in the spectrum of N-CDs + DOX; these peaks corresponded to the N–H groups [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake and drug release in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the cell uptake of N-CDs + DOX, we treated HeLa cells with N-CDs + DOX and free DOX, and the uptake and drug release processes were assessed via flow cytometry based on the intrinsic fluorescence of DOX. Experimental results revealed that DOX released from N-CDs + DOX efficiently enters into the HeLa cells (Fig. S10), suggesting that the N-CDs may serve as an efficient nanocarrier for the intracellular delivery of DOX. The detailed information was explained in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single- and two-photon fluorescence imaging in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cell uptake and intracellular drug tracking were also confirmed in the laser scanning confocal microscopy images. The green fluorescence came from N-CDs upon excitation at 405 nm and the red fluorescence came from DOX released from N-CDs + DOX upon excitation at 488 nm (in Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6a, green emission was shown in the cell membrane and cytoplasm, whereas the red emission (Fig. 6b) was mainly distributed in the cytoplasm, indicating that after the 0.5 h N-CDs began to enter the cytoplasm, DOX (was released form N-CDs + DOX), had entered the cytoplasm, and started to gather into nucleus. After 4 h incubation, N-CDs moved into the nucleus, whereas DOX was significantly concentrated in the nucleus.

N-CDs also exhibited remarkable TPFI capability, which is attributed to its strong absorption in the near-infrared region (800–1100 nm). More recently, TPFI has been considered as a promising strategy, and its luminescence mechanism allows for higher resolution, deep penetration, low photobleaching, low background signal, and low cell damage compared with SPFI [51,52]. To adjust the absorption and emission and meet the requirements of TPFI, an elaborate structural design and chemical composition are needed, and limited carbon materials have been reported to possess the TPFI performance [53]. In this study, we found that the prepared N-CDs with such charge distribution in structure and chemical bonds in composition enjoy this outstanding performance, and was used to demonstrate the effective endocytosis of N-CDs in HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 6d and 6 h, an 800 nm laser with lower energy, less damage to the cells and deeper penetration was acted as the excitation source, and the obtained images exhibited high contrast TPFI of N-CDs distributed in the HeLa cells, which were much clearer than SPFI. Therefore N-CDs can be applied to cell imaging and deep tissue imaging, and more importantly, the designed N-CDs had small size and negligible toxicity (Fig. 7 a), endowing them with distinctly novel advantages over other imaging reagents. To exclude the effects of DOX on TPFI imaging, pure DOX and N-CDs were excited by 800 nm wavelength to study their fluorescence property (Fig. S11), and we found that only the N-CDs exhibited bright emission, whereas DOX showed no fluorescence under 800-nm excitation. To further demonstrate the bioimaging function, N-CDs were directly used to cell imaging, and the corresponding results are provided in Fig S12, which displays the bright field image, TPFI, and merged image of HeLa cells treated with N-CDs for 0.5 h. N-CDs were easily internalized by the HeLa cells because of their smaller size and hydrophilic surface. To show the deep-tissue bioimaging performance, the imaging effects of N-CDs under different depths were studied, and we found that a high depth of 18 μm was readily obtained (Fig. S13) [54]. These results indicated that N-CDs have an excellent potential in monitoring the release of drug in real-time and can be used as a novel imaging reagent for deep-tissue imaging.

The N-CDs showed excellent fluorescent properties, whereas their small size, high water solubility, and rich functional groups on the surface endowed them the potential as a nanocarrier. To study this performance, MTT tests were used to assess the tumor inhibition function of N-CDs + DOX [55]. For biomedical applications, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs should be taken into account. As shown in Fig. 7a, the viabilities of different cells including HeLa cells, and 16HBE cells, treated with N-CDs (10–200 μg/mL) were investigated. The results revealed that N-CDs showed no cytotoxicity toward HeLa cells and 16HBE cells under experimental conditions, even when the concentration reached a high value of 200 μg/mL [56]. Fig. 7b shows the cell viability after the cells were treated with N-CDs + DOX and free DOX for 24 h, with increasing doses of DOX from 1 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL. The inhibition ratio of N-CDs + DOX were significantly enhanced, especially to about 62% at a DOX dose of 20 μg/mL. The inhibition ratio of free DOX was enhanced to 81% at the same concentration. Free DOX is well-known to often exhibit higher inhibition ratio compared with nanocarriers [57], and this effect is mostly ascribed to the easy diffusion of DOX. For the cells treated after 48 h, N-CDs + DOX revealed higher cell inhibition ratio (increased to 75%) than the cells treated after 24 h (Fig. 7c). Similar results were obtained from Fig. 7d, N-CDs + DOX and free DOX exhibited similar inhibitory effects, especially at higher concentrations, which are mostly owed to the cellular uptake. These results suggested that N-CDs, as a novel transmembrane delivery nanocarrier facilitated cellular uptake and intracellular drug accumulation, both of which can enhance drug toxicity and revealed a high tumor inhibition at low drug concentration.

We have successfully synthesized a water-soluble, chemically stable, lowly toxic, and highly fluorescent N-CDs using one-pot synthesis strategy; the N-CDs gave a high fluorescence yield of about 45%. Based on these advantages, we can develop a facile, reliable, and effective IFE-based N-CDs fluorescence sensor platform for βG activity sensing for human serum and intracellular detection. The fluorescence assay was demonstrated to be a facile and sensitive method, with a detection limit of 0.3 U/L and provided a potential platform for βG activity detection in clinical tumor diagnosis. More importantly, we also demonstrated N-CDs as a novel nanocarrier to visually monitor drug loading via FRET and monitor and track the release of DOX in HeLa cells in real-time. N-CDs had been demonstrated to be capable of two-photon imaging and monitoring in deep tissues. The MTT assay against 16HBE normal cells and HeLa cells verified the low cytotoxicity of N-CDs. N-CDs + DOX was also easily internalized by the HeLa cells, and high-quality single- and two-photon imaging enabled real-time monitoring of the endocytosis, drug-release and intracellular distribution, leading to enhanced cancer therapy with a low dose of DOX. These results suggest a significant potential of the method for biomedical and bioimaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are very grateful for the financial support from Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018QB002, ZR2018MB038, ZR2016BQ13), and Higher Educational Science and Technology Program of Shandong Province (J16LA03). We also thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671118), Taishan Scholar Foundation of Shandong Province as well as Doctoral Start-Up Scientific Research Foundation (BSQD20150114) of Qufu Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.09.100.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
